summary: An American philosophical tradition founded by C.S. Peirce and William James. Emphasizes practical consequences of ideas as the measure of their truth. Key works include James's Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking. Later figures include John Dewey and Richard Rorty (neopragmatism).

works.name: Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking (William James)
works.description: William James's 1907 lecture series presenting pragmatism as a philosophical method. The book distinguishes between 'tender-minded' rationalists and 'tough-minded' empiricists, introduces the idea that truth has 'cash value in experiential terms,' and uses the famous squirrel-around-a-tree anecdote to show that the pragmatic method resolves metaphysical disputes by asking what 'practically means.' It also discusses Walt Whitman's 'To You' in monistic vs. pluralistic readings, offers 'melioristic theism' as a middle ground, and examines eight ways the world could be 'unified.'

clues.clue: squirrel-around-a-tree anecdote — resolves dispute by asking what 'going round' practically means
examples: quibbles with the definition of 'going around' in a discussion of a man chasing a squirrel around a tree

works.name: C.S. Peirce & the Founding of Pragmatism
works.description: Charles Sanders Peirce is the 'father of pragmatism' who wrote the foundational essays 'How to Make Our Ideas Clear' and 'The Fixation of Belief' for Popular Science Monthly. He formulated the pragmatic maxim (consider the practical effects of your thinking), introduced the methods of tenacity and authority for settling opinion, advocated for the scientific method, and developed abductive reasoning and triadic semiotics. William James credited Peirce when first publishing the term 'pragmatism' in 1898.

clues.clue: Peirce founded pragmatism; 'How to Make Our Ideas Clear' and 'The Fixation of Belief'
examples: this school of thought of John Dewey, Charles Sanders Peirce, and William James
Peirce is considered to have founded this American school of philosophy

clues.clue: pragmatic maxim — consider the 'practical effects' of your thinking; three grades of clearness
examples: Peirce wrote this school's maxim to always consider the 'practical effects' of your thinking
discusses the example of adding accelerations together using a parallelogram

examples: the concepts of a 'community of inquiry' and 'abductive reasoning' were introduced by a logician from this movement

clues.clue: criticized transubstantiation as 'senseless jargon'
examples: a thinker from this movement criticized the doctrine of transubstantiation as 'senseless jargon'

works.name: Richard Rorty & Neopragmatism
works.description: Richard Rorty revived pragmatism in its 'neo' form, combining it with analytic philosophy's focus on language. His Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature (1979) promotes 'epistemological behaviorism' and attacks representational theories of knowledge. Contingency, Irony, and Solidarity defines 'ironists' who doubt their 'final vocabulary.' Achieving Our Country favorably contrasts the progressive Left with the 'spectatorial' academic Left. Susan Haack has argued Rorty did not truly belong to pragmatism.

clues.clue: Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature — promotes 'epistemological behaviorism'; attacks representational theories
examples: this book advocating what he called 'epistemological behaviorism'
this book promotes 'epistemological behaviorism' in opposition to traditional accounts

examples: this thinker's book Contingency, Irony, and Solidarity discusses 'ironists,' who perpetually doubt their 'final vocabulary'

clues.clue: Achieving Our Country — praises progressive Left; criticizes spectatorial academic Left
examples: Achieving Our Country favorably contrasts the agency of the progressive Left prior to the Vietnam War with the new 'spectatorial' Left

examples: this tradition was combined with analytic philosophy's focus on language to create its 'neo' form in Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature

works.name: John Dewey & Democracy and Education
works.description: John Dewey is the third major pragmatist, known for his philosophy of education (Democracy and Education), his aesthetics (Art as Experience), and his political philosophy (The Public and Its Problems). Dewey called his philosophy 'instrumentalism.' Cheryl Misak's history of pragmatism includes Jane Addams as an influence on Dewey.

clues.clue: Dewey — Democracy and Education, instrumentalism, third major pragmatist
examples: Cheryl Misak's history of this philosophical school of thought includes Jane Addams, who influenced the author of Democracy and Education

clues.clue: Dewey influenced B.R. Ambedkar at Columbia
examples: this thinker who sought 'a bridge between Dewey and Buddhism' as one of Dewey's students at Columbia

works.name: Louis Menand & The Metaphysical Club
works.description: Louis Menand's book The Metaphysical Club traces the origins of pragmatism. The 'Metaphysical Club' was a discussion group at Harvard that included Peirce, James, and Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. Susan Haack and the Pittsburgh School (Robert Brandom, Wilfrid Sellars, John McDowell) are modern pragmatists.

clues.clue: Louis Menand's The Metaphysical Club traces origins of pragmatism
examples: Louis Menand traced the origins of this tradition in his book The Metaphysical Club

clues.clue: Pittsburgh School — Brandom, Sellars, McDowell; analytic pragmatism
examples: Robert Brandom and Wilfrid Sellars are considered a part of the neo- form of this philosophical tradition

clues.clue: Susan Haack — foundherentism, defended Peirce against Rorty's interpretation
examples: Susan Haack questioned whether one philosopher belonged to this school

works.name: General / Identification
works.description: Pragmatism is an American philosophical tradition that judges ideas by their practical consequences. Its three founders are Peirce, James, and Dewey. It is a 'new name for some old ways of thinking.'

clues.clue: American philosophical tradition of Peirce, James, and Dewey
examples: what school of thought of John Dewey, Charles Sanders Peirce, and William James?

clues.clue: judges ideas by practical consequences / practical applications
examples: this school of philosophy attempted to judge ideas by looking at their practical consequences

clues.clue: Habermas's thought on truth inspired by pragmatism
examples: Habermas's thought on truth was inspired by this school of U.S. thought

clues.clue: Kyoto School's Nishida adopted 'pure experience' from William James
examples: Nishida adopted his idea of 'pure experience' from this American philosopher

comprehensive_summary:
Pragmatism is the dominant American philosophical tradition, founded by Charles Sanders Peirce and developed by William James and John Dewey. It holds that the meaning and truth of ideas should be judged by their practical consequences. Peirce formulated the pragmatic maxim in 'How to Make Our Ideas Clear' and advocated the scientific method over the methods of tenacity and authority in 'The Fixation of Belief.'

William James's 1907 lecture series Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking is the most heavily clued work on pragmatism. It distinguishes between 'tender-minded' rationalists and 'tough-minded' empiricists, introduces the idea that truth has 'cash value,' and uses the famous squirrel-around-a-tree anecdote to show that the pragmatic method resolves metaphysical disputes. The book also discusses carving constellations from the flux of reality, Walt Whitman's 'To You' in pluralistic and monistic readings, and proposes 'melioristic theism.'

Richard Rorty revived pragmatism in its 'neo' form, combining it with analytic philosophy in Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature. His Achieving Our Country praises the progressive Left and criticizes the spectatorial academic Left. Susan Haack has argued Rorty did not truly belong to pragmatism. The Pittsburgh School (Robert Brandom, Wilfrid Sellars, John McDowell) represents modern analytic pragmatism. Louis Menand traced the origins of pragmatism in The Metaphysical Club. Pragmatism also influenced thinkers abroad: Nishida and the Kyoto School adopted James's concept of 'pure experience,' and B.R. Ambedkar studied under Dewey at Columbia.
